title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Theoretical Analysis of the Label Trick in Graph Neural Networks

## Summary

This paper provides a formal theoretical treatment of the label trick, a heuristic technique widely used in graph neural network competitions and benchmarks. While the authors offer rigorous mathematical analysis and introduce interpretable simplifications of the method, the empirical validation of the proposed applications is notably weak. The work raises important questions about why the label trick remains popular in practice, but does not adequately answer them through either theoretical insight or experimental evidence.

## Strengths

1. **Solid theoretical foundation**: The paper successfully formalizes the label trick, which has previously been employed primarily as an ad-hoc heuristic without rigorous justification. This theoretical contribution is valuable for the community.

2. **Clear mathematical decomposition**: The authors derive an explicit decomposition showing that the label trick induces both a data-fitting term and a graph-dependent regularization factor. This decomposition provides useful intuition about the method's behavior.

3. **Interpretable simplification**: The introduction of a self-excluded variant that is amenable to theoretical interpretation represents a useful contribution for understanding the mechanism at work.

4. **Broader applicability**: The paper discusses how the label trick can enable trainable weights in previously parameter-free or non-end-to-end methods, suggesting potential for broader impact.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Weak empirical validation of use-cases**: The experimental results presented in Tables 1-4 (Sections 5.1-5.4) demonstrate marginal or negligible improvements over baseline methods. When standard deviations across runs are considered, many reported gains fall within noise margins and lack statistical significance. This substantially undermines the practical motivation for the proposed applications.

2. **Missing ablation studies**: The paper does not include ablation studies on top-performing models from the Open Graph Benchmark and KDDCUP 2021 Challenge that directly compare performance with and without the label trick. Such studies would be essential for validating whether the label trick is necessary or merely one of many contributing factors.

3. **Incomplete analysis of practical popularity**: The paper observes that top leaderboard submissions employ the label trick but does not investigate why. The authors do not explore alternative explanations for its widespread adoption—such as faster convergence, improved training stability, or robustness properties—that might explain its popularity independent of final accuracy gains.

4. **Disconnect between theory and practice**: The focus on theoretical analysis is problematic when empirical results are weak. The paper does not clarify when or why the label trick provides substantial practical benefits, leaving the relationship between theoretical insights and real-world utility unclear.

## Technical Concerns

- **Statistical significance**: Standard deviations reported across ten independent runs are large enough that the improvements shown for trainable label propagation (Table 1) and deterministic label trick for linear GNNs (Table 3) are often within noise.

- **Confounding factors**: The paper does not adequately separate the contribution of the label trick from other training techniques, hyperparameter choices, and implementation details that may influence results.

- **Mixed results**: Results for trainable C&S (Table 4) are particularly concerning, with some datasets showing no improvement in test accuracy despite gains in validation accuracy, suggesting potential overfitting or dataset-specific effects.

## Minor Weaknesses

The justification for the label trick's popularity based solely on leaderboard rankings is superficial and does not constitute evidence that the label trick itself is responsible for success. The novel use-cases motivated by the theoretical analysis lack compelling empirical validation to justify their practical utility.

## Suggestions for Improvement

1. Conduct rigorous ablation studies on established benchmarks comparing top-performing models with and without the label trick to establish its necessity.

2. Investigate additional properties beyond accuracy, including convergence speed, training stability, and robustness across different initialization schemes and hyperparameters.

3. Provide more compelling empirical evidence for the proposed use-cases with clear performance gains over well-tuned baselines.

4. Analyze the conditions under which the label trick provides substantial improvements versus marginal gains, clarifying the gap between theory and practice.

5. Address the fundamental question: if empirical improvements are marginal, what explains the label trick's widespread adoption?

## Overall Assessment

This paper makes a solid theoretical contribution by formalizing and analyzing the label trick. However, it falls short of demonstrating that this theoretical understanding translates into practical benefits. The empirical validation is weak, with most improvements falling within standard deviation margins, and critical ablation studies are missing. The paper identifies an interesting phenomenon (the label trick's popularity) but does not adequately explain it. Despite these limitations, the theoretical analysis itself has merit and the paper raises important questions about understanding why certain techniques become popular in practice.

The work is marginally above the acceptance threshold. The theoretical contributions are sufficient to warrant publication, but the weak empirical results and incomplete analysis of practical benefits prevent this from being a strong accept. The paper would benefit significantly from addressing the empirical validation and providing deeper insights into the label trick's practical utility.

**Recommendation: Accept (marginally)**

Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

Summary Of The Review:
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
# Peer Review

## Summary

This paper presents a rigorous theoretical analysis of the label trick in graph neural networks, transforming what has been a heuristically-motivated technique into a well-founded theoretical framework. The authors derive general formulations showing how the label trick functions as a regularizer, introduce novel applications motivated by their theoretical insights, and validate these applications with promising empirical results. This work makes a valuable contribution by filling an important theoretical gap in the literature.

## Strengths

1. **Strong Theoretical Foundation**: The paper provides much-needed rigorous justification for the label trick, which has been widely adopted in practice but lacked formal theoretical grounding. This is a significant contribution that elevates the understanding of an important technique.

2. **Clear and Rigorous Presentation**: The derivations are presented smoothly and systematically, making the theoretical analysis accessible without sacrificing rigor. The authors effectively guide the reader through both the general formulations and specific instantiations of their framework.

3. **Comprehensive Coverage**: The work addresses both regression (MSE loss) and classification (categorical cross-entropy) cases, demonstrating the generality of their approach. The analysis extends to both linear and nonlinear settings, further strengthening the breadth of the contribution.

4. **Insightful Analysis**: Beyond the formal derivations, the paper contains several thought-provoking remarks and conclusions that provide deeper intuition into why the label trick works and how it can be leveraged. These insights naturally motivate the novel use cases presented.

5. **Theory-Driven Innovation**: Rather than proposing methods ad hoc, the authors identify novel applications of the label trick directly from their theoretical analysis. The empirical validation of these use cases is convincing and demonstrates the practical value of the theoretical insights.

6. **Addresses a Real Gap**: By providing theoretical justification for a widely-adopted heuristic, this work serves the community by establishing solid foundations for future research and practice in this area.

## Weaknesses

The reviewer notes limited expertise in this particular domain, which may constrain the depth of technical assessment. However, within the scope of the review, no significant technical flaws or concerns were identified.

## Recommendation

This is a solid theoretical contribution that successfully bridges the gap between heuristic practice and rigorous theory. The clear presentation, comprehensive analysis, and well-motivated novel applications make this a valuable addition to the literature. The empirical validation further strengthens the contribution.

**I recommend acceptance of this paper.**

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Connecting Label Propagation and Message Passing Neural Networks

## Summary

This paper investigates the theoretical foundations of the "label trick," a heuristic technique used in graph neural networks that combines label and feature propagation. The authors provide a formal analysis connecting label propagation to message passing frameworks, with particular attention to how removing diagonal terms prevents trivial identity solutions. While the paper addresses a fundamental problem that deserves rigorous treatment, it falls short of the acceptance threshold due to presentation issues, incomplete comparisons with related work, and gaps between theory and practice.

## Strengths

The paper tackles an important and timely problem. Understanding the theoretical underpinnings of the label trick would provide valuable insights to the community, as this technique has been employed primarily as a heuristic without rigorous justification. The authors deserve credit for recognizing this gap and attempting to formalize the approach.

The proposed fix to the propagation matrix—removing diagonal terms in Equation (10)—is intuitive and well-motivated. This modification elegantly prevents trivial solutions where the model simply learns the identity mapping, making the subsequent theoretical analysis meaningful.

The work appropriately positions itself as filling a gap in the literature by moving beyond informal use of the label trick toward systematic theoretical understanding.

## Major Weaknesses

**Insufficient Attribution and Comparison with Prior Work**: The paper does not adequately credit or compare against prior work that has explored similar ideas. Specifically, the reviewer notes that arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf appears to have first proposed the label trick, yet receives insufficient discussion. More critically, the cited work by Jia & Benson (2021) on comparing label and feature propagation is not thoroughly compared against, despite being directly relevant. The paper would benefit from a more comprehensive positioning relative to these and other works on learning or modifying propagation matrices.

**Clarity and Notation Issues**: The paper suffers from dense notation that impedes readability. The subscript E_splits is particularly vague, and excessive subscripting throughout makes following the mathematical exposition challenging. Beyond notation, key intuitions are missing. For instance, Equation (3)'s motivation—why post-multiplying Y_tr with a weight matrix W is desirable—is not clearly explained. These presentation issues significantly hinder accessibility.

**Unclear and Incomplete Derivations**: The derivation of Equation (5) lacks clarity, particularly the transition from the second-to-last to the final term. The relationship between the expectation over splits and the deterministic form when |D_out| = 1 requires better explanation. Additionally, the inconsistent use of parentheses versus square brackets in Equations (4) and (5) suggests insufficient polish.

**Theory-Practice Gap**: The theoretical analysis assumes simplifying conditions—no node features and linear models—that substantially limit generalizability. The connection between these theoretical results and the nonlinear GNN experiments is not rigorously established. Theorem 3's extension to nonlinear cases only characterizes extreme scenarios (α→0 and α→1) and acknowledges potential discrepancies between training and inference, which undermines confidence in the practical applicability of the theory.

## Minor Weaknesses and Technical Concerns

**Section 4.1 Lacks Detail**: The trainable label propagation discussion is underdeveloped. The training procedure, final model specification, and how the derived regularization term is actually incorporated into practice remain unclear. It is also unclear which specific GNN architecture is used in Figure 1 (right plot) and why the deterministic label trick is not included for comparison.

**Experimental and Methodological Issues**: 
- Table 2 should explicitly use R² metric for regression evaluation rather than other metrics.
- It is unclear whether the regularization term Γ = (diag(P^T P) - C^T C)^(1/2) Y_tr derived in theory is actually used in Table 3 results. If omitted, the justification should be provided.
- The paper does not clearly specify whether stochastic or deterministic versions of the label trick are used in each experiment.
- Remark 4's claim that no overfitting occurs when regularization is disabled lacks mechanistic explanation.

**Technical Details**: The scaling factor 1/α in Equation (7) is introduced to maintain mean and variance consistency, but its necessity and practical impact deserve better explanation. The regularization factor Γ depends on the propagation matrix P, yet the paper provides no guidance on choosing P in practice.

**Writing Quality**: Beyond notation, the prose could be improved. Remarks interrupt the flow, and the paper reads as dense and difficult to parse. A comprehensive revision focusing on readability is needed. Additionally, there is a typo in Remark 2 where "insure" should be "ensure."

## Missing Elements

The paper does not discuss references to other work on learning or modifying adjacency and propagation matrices, which would strengthen the positioning of this contribution. The practical implications of Theorem 1's decomposition into data-fitting and regularization terms are underexplored. Finally, clearer guidance on when and how to apply the regularization interpretation in practice would enhance the paper's utility.

## Questions for Authors

1. How does this work relate to the label trick as proposed in arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf, and what are the key novel contributions beyond that work?
2. Can you provide a direct empirical comparison with Jia & Benson (2021)?
3. Is the derived regularization term actually used in the experiments, and if not, why?
4. How should practitioners choose the propagation matrix P when applying the regularization framework?

## Recommendation

While the paper addresses a relevant problem and provides some theoretical insights, it requires substantial improvements before publication. The presentation is dense and difficult to follow, key derivations lack clarity, comparisons with related work are insufficient, and the gap between theory (linear models, no features) and practice (nonlinear GNNs) is not adequately bridged. The work would benefit from a thorough revision emphasizing clarity, more rigorous comparisons with prior work, and clearer guidance on practical application. In its current form, the contribution is marginally below the acceptance threshold.

**Decision: Marginally Below Acceptance Threshold (5/10)**

The paper makes contributions to an important problem but needs significant revisions in presentation, comparison with related work, and bridging the theory-practice gap before it can be recommended for acceptance.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Add clearer intuitive explanation for why post-multiplying Y_tr with weight matrix W is desirable in Equation (3)
Include direct comparisons with Jia & Benson 2021 and other works comparing label and feature propagation approaches

main_points: The paper addresses an important but previously informal topic - the theoretical understanding of the label trick used in GNNs
The theoretical analysis assumes simplifying conditions (no node features, linear models) that may limit generalizability
The connection to classical label propagation is valuable but needs better positioning relative to prior work
The deterministic self-excluded simplification (Equation 6) is a useful theoretical tool but its practical utility is unclear
The regularization interpretation of the label trick is interesting but the paper lacks clear guidance on when and how to apply it in practice
Broader applications (trainable label propagation, trainable C&S) are presented but lack sufficient implementation and motivation details
technical_details: The derivation in Equation (5) needs clarification on how the expectation over splits reduces to a deterministic form when |D_out| = 1
Theorem 1 provides a decomposition into data-fitting and regularization terms, but the practical implications are not fully explored
The scaling factor 1/α in Equation (7) is introduced to maintain consistent mean and variance but its necessity and impact should be better explained
Theorem 2 provides an upper bound for cross-entropy loss but only loosely aligns with the MSE-based theory
Theorem 3 for nonlinear extensions only characterizes extreme cases (α→0 and α→1) and acknowledges that test node predictions may differ during training vs inference
The connection between theoretical results (which assume linear models) and experimental results (which use nonlinear GNNs) is not rigorously established
The regularization factor Γ = (diag(P^T P) - C^T C)^(1/2) Y_tr depends on the propagation matrix P but the paper does not discuss how to choose P in practice
The paper uses both stochastic (label trick S) and deterministic (label trick D) versions but does not clearly specify which is used in each experiment

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Appendix:
There is several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

References formatting:
ArXiv -> journal and conference publications

I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

reviews.text:
# Peer Review: Theoretical Analysis of the Label Trick for Graph Neural Networks

## Summary

This paper attempts to provide theoretical understanding of the label trick in graph neural networks by analyzing it through simpler models and exploring novel applications. While the authors tackle an interesting question about understanding practical heuristics, the execution falls short of publication standards. The theoretical contributions lack rigor and sufficient experimental validation, and fundamental assumptions about the problem setup contain unresolved logical inconsistencies.

## Strengths

1. **Valuable Research Direction**: The paper addresses a worthwhile goal—moving beyond treating the label trick as an engineering heuristic to provide theoretical grounding. This perspective is commendable and could yield important insights.

2. **Presentation Quality**: The paper is generally well-written and relatively easy to follow, which facilitates engagement with the material.

## Major Weaknesses

### 1. Insufficient Theoretical Rigor and Validation

The central theoretical claim—that the label trick can be understood through a duality between stochasticity and regularization—is not adequately supported. While the paper presents this duality as a key insight, the experimental validation is insufficient and lacks the targeted design needed to directly test this theoretical prediction. The paper would benefit from experiments explicitly designed to validate each component of the theoretical analysis.

### 2. Fundamental Logical Inconsistencies

The paper contains several unresolved contradictions that undermine its theoretical foundation:

- **Graph Construction Paradox**: The authors assume that graphs are constructed from feature similarity, yet claim to analyze settings where features are unreliable or noisy. If features are indeed unreliable, then graphs built from feature similarity would inherit this unreliability. This contradiction is never addressed.

- **Feature Independence Assumption**: The claim that the analysis applies when features are noisy or uninformative is logically inconsistent with using those same features to construct the graph structure.

These issues suggest that the problem formulation itself needs reconsideration.

### 3. Incomplete Comparative Analysis

A critical gap is the lack of rigorous comparison with existing regularization methods. The paper does not explain why the label trick outperforms:
- Laplacian regularization (Pfau et al., Cabannes et al.)
- MixUp-based regularization (Lopez-Paz et al.)
- Other standard semi-supervised learning approaches

Without such comparisons, the claimed advantages remain unsubstantiated. This is particularly problematic given that the paper's main contribution is supposedly to explain *why* the label trick works.

### 4. Missing Insights on Key Phenomena

The paper fails to provide intuition for important practical observations:
- Why does self-excluded prediction (where a node's own label is excluded from the smoothing) perform worse than the full label trick?
- How does the label trick relate to other semi-supervised learning paradigms?

These questions are central to understanding the method, yet they remain unanswered.

## Minor Weaknesses and Technical Issues

### Definitional and Notational Problems

1. **Equation (1)**: The second occurrence of F should be F_tr to avoid penalizing test data. Additionally, the paper should explicitly specify that F ∈ ℝ^{n×c} and clarify that the objective can be minimized independently per coordinate.

2. **Page 3 Notation**: The approximation P ≈ PY needs clarification. Since λS is contracting, this should be presented as a convergent infinite series rather than a simple approximation.

3. **Propagation Matrix Definition**: The definition of P in Equation (3) is vague. The paper must explicitly specify how P is fixed and what role it plays in the analysis.

4. **Abstract Claims**: The claim about "identity mapping" in the abstract lacks precision. This should be explicitly restricted to the transductive setting where features are effectively ignored.

### Graph Formalism

The exposition of the graph formalism is insufficient:
- The relationship between node features (X ∈ ℝ^d), labels (Y ∈ ℝ^c), and the adjacency matrix needs clarification.
- The paper discusses label smoothness over connected nodes but does not specify whether the graph is weighted or unweighted. This distinction is crucial since label smoothness properties depend on edge weights.

### Missing Motivation and Context

- The choice to use the SGC (Simplified Graph Convolution) model lacks motivation. Why is this particular model chosen for analysis? How do results generalize to other architectures?
- The distinction between i.i.d. samples and smooth label variation over graphs could be explained more clearly.
- Sections 3.2 and 3.3 appear somewhat condensed relative to their importance, potentially at the expense of clarity.

### Presentation Issues

- LaTeX formatting problems (overboxes) appear in the appendix and should be corrected.
- References should be updated from ArXiv preprints to final journal/conference publications where available.

## Missing Elements

1. **Targeted Experiments**: Design experiments that directly validate the theoretical duality claims rather than only demonstrating empirical performance.

2. **Comparative Baselines**: Include systematic comparisons with Laplacian regularization and MixUp approaches to establish relative advantages.

3. **Ablation Studies**: Provide clearer ablation studies that isolate the contributions of different components.

## Questions for Authors

1. How do you reconcile the assumption that features are unreliable with the use of feature similarity for graph construction?
2. Why is the label trick superior to existing regularization methods? Can you provide a principled explanation beyond empirical performance?
3. What causes self-excluded prediction to underperform, and what does this reveal about the mechanism of the label trick?

## Recommendation

While this paper addresses an interesting question about understanding practical methods in GNNs, the execution is insufficient for publication at a top venue. The theoretical contributions lack the rigor and insight needed to justify the main claims, fundamental assumptions contain unresolved logical inconsistencies, and the experimental validation is not targeted toward testing the theoretical predictions. The absence of rigorous comparisons with alternative methods further weakens the contribution.

The paper would benefit from:
1. Resolving the logical inconsistencies in the problem formulation
2. Conducting targeted experiments to validate theoretical claims
3. Providing rigorous comparisons with existing regularization approaches
4. Offering deeper insights into why the label trick works and when it should be preferred

With substantial revisions addressing these concerns, this work could make a solid contribution. In its current form, however, it falls marginally below the acceptance threshold.

**Decision: Reject (marginally below acceptance threshold)**

And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Appendix:
There is several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

References formatting:
ArXiv -> journal and conference publications

Summary Of The Review:
Overall while having justifications of what people do in practice is nice, I think this paper fall short of providing a satisfactory analysis of the label tricks, especially since it does not rigorously explained it superiority compared to other methods.
I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Peer Review: [Paper Title]

## Summary

This paper proposes a deterministic alternative to the stochastic label trick for graph neural networks, replacing it with a self-exclusion operator-based approach to prevent label leakage during training. While the work presents a novel theoretical framework and contributes valuable theoretical analysis through Theorem 2, the paper falls short of making a convincing case for its practical adoption due to insufficient experimental validation and unclear motivation for the proposed changes.

## Strengths

1. **Novel Theoretical Contribution**: The paper introduces an interesting deterministic framework based on the self-exclusion operator as an alternative to the stochastic label trick. This represents a genuine contribution to understanding label propagation mechanisms in graph neural networks.

2. **Rigorous Theoretical Analysis**: Theorem 2 provides a valuable upper bound on the deterministic proposal with respect to cross-entropy loss, offering theoretical substantiation for the label trick—an aspect that has previously lacked rigorous mathematical treatment in the literature.

3. **Addresses a Literature Gap**: By providing theoretical grounding for the label trick, the paper fills an important void in the literature and contributes to our understanding of why this technique works.

## Major Weaknesses

1. **Unconvincing Motivation**: The primary motivation for replacing the stochastic label trick with a deterministic alternative is not clearly articulated. Beyond sampling considerations, the paper fails to explain why practitioners should prefer the deterministic version. Computational bottlenecks and implementation challenges are not discussed, leaving readers uncertain about the practical rationale for this change.

2. **Lack of Direct Experimental Comparison**: The paper provides only limited direct comparison between the proposed deterministic label trick and state-of-the-art stochastic label trick implementations (essentially only Figure 1). This is a critical omission given that demonstrating superiority over existing methods is essential for justifying the proposed approach.

3. **Marginal and Non-Significant Improvements**: The experimental improvements reported are marginal when standard deviations are considered. The gains shown in comparisons (e.g., trainable label propagation versus traditional methods, with versus without label trick) do not appear statistically significant, raising questions about the practical utility of the proposed method.

4. **Insufficient Statistical Rigor**: The experimental validation lacks rigorous statistical analysis. Given the reported standard deviations, many claimed improvements fall within the margin of error, yet this uncertainty is not adequately acknowledged or discussed.

5. **Tangential Experimental Focus**: Rather than directly validating the core theoretical contribution, the experiments focus on comparisons that are tangential to the main contribution (e.g., trainable versus non-trainable label propagation). This misalignment between theory and experimental validation weakens the overall contribution.

## Minor Weaknesses

1. **Incomplete Literature Review**: Important references on label propagation in graphs are missing, notably Szlam et al. (2008) on regularization on graphs with function-adapted diffusion processes. This omission suggests an incomplete engagement with the broader literature.

2. **Insufficient Algorithm Explanation**: Many algorithms mentioned in the paper are not fully explained or presented in a self-contained manner, making the paper difficult to follow. The reference to C&S (Correct and Smooth) is particularly problematic, as it is not properly introduced.

3. **Presentation Issues**: The paper would benefit from clearer exposition of key concepts and more detailed algorithmic descriptions to aid reader comprehension.

## Specific Suggestions

1. Clearly articulate the motivation for the deterministic approach beyond sampling considerations. Discuss computational implications, implementation advantages, or other practical benefits that would justify the proposed change.

2. Provide comprehensive direct experimental comparisons between the proposed deterministic label trick and state-of-the-art stochastic implementations, with results prominently featured in the main paper.

3. Demonstrate concrete practical advantages—whether in terms of accuracy improvements, computational speed-up, or other metrics—that would justify adoption of the deterministic approach.

4. Conduct more rigorous statistical analysis of experimental results. Report confidence intervals, perform significance tests, and explicitly discuss whether observed improvements exceed the margin of error defined by standard deviations.

5. Expand the literature review to include relevant work on label propagation and graph regularization, particularly the missing references noted above.

6. Provide more self-contained explanations of key algorithms and methods referenced in the paper, especially C&S and other baseline methods.

7. Refocus experimental validation to directly test the main theoretical contributions rather than conducting tangential comparisons.

## Overall Assessment

While this paper makes a theoretical contribution by providing rigorous analysis of the label trick and proposing a deterministic alternative, it does not sufficiently demonstrate why this alternative should be preferred in practice. The experimental section fails to provide convincing evidence of practical advantages, with most improvements appearing marginal and statistically insignificant. The motivation for the proposed changes remains unclear, and the paper lacks direct comparisons with existing state-of-the-art methods. Additionally, incomplete literature coverage and presentation issues further weaken the contribution.

The paper would be significantly strengthened by addressing the motivation gap, providing rigorous experimental validation with statistical significance testing, and conducting direct comparisons with existing methods. In its current form, the work is not ready for publication at a top venue.

## Recommendation

**Marginally Below Acceptance Threshold** – The paper presents interesting theoretical ideas but requires substantial improvements in experimental validation, motivation clarity, and comparative analysis before it can make a compelling case for publication.

title: Imitation Learning by Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper shows that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning (IL) could be reduced to RL with a stationary reward. The paper thus provides theoretical grounding for several existing works. In addition, the paper also draws connection with f-divergence minimization commonly found in adversarial IL. On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
